channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.thesixthaxis.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.thesixthaxis.com

item.title: Crabmeat Review – Claws and Effect
item.content:encoded:
Crabs are terrifying creatures, so it makes perfect sense for them to be the central focus of a Lovecraftian horror game. Crabmeat takes the skittering claws and beady eyes of the crustacean and forces you into direct contact in a dystopian world that feels exposed and alien. Billed as a point ‘n’ click survival horror, the entirely mouse-driven first-person game succeeds in making you feel uncomfortable and presenting a nightmare future in which being in debt is a life sentence.

You wake up on a crabbing boat alone and tasked with catching enough crabs to pay off your debt to the Australian feudostate. Venturing out into the harsh and icy seas of the Antarctic, you have a strict time limit in which to fulfil your quota, with a fatal punishment threatened if you don’t succeed. The threat is existential but also physical in the form of mutated denizens of the sea that regularly board the boat and must be fought off.

Graphically, Crabmeat has a crunchy PS2-style aesthetic that helps to cement the uncanny feeling and uneasiness. Everything is clearly and literally signposted with instructions for the various machines you’ll need to use around the environment. The design of the boat is almost like a mini Dark Souls level with locked doors and shortcuts to discover. The initial air of mystery is replaced by a deep familiarity with the layout and responsibilities of single-handedly piloting the boat and manning the equipment.

The beginning half hour or so of the game is mainly a process of learning how to navigate, bait the crabpots, hook them, and winch them back in. Once you have your catch, you have to manually sort through it to only keep the larger crabs, with everything else being released back into the ocean. There is a tactility to everything that feels almost like a VR game controlled entirely by mouse – a control system that is already proving controversial, going by Steam comments about the demo version. Many are annoyed that there is no WASD movement control, but the more I played, the more I appreciated the uniqueness of the controls and the ways in which they fit with the restrictive nature of your punishment on the boat.

Navigating the boat takes a little getting used to, as there is obviously a much larger turning circle than you’d get in more traditional vehicles. Once it clicks, however, you’ll be swinging around and getting in place to collect the crab pots as soon as they are filled. To do so, you have to harpoon them and then winch them in with the harpoon being on the opposite side of the boat to where you drop them off. This simple mechanic makes the repetitive process feel engaging, and there is a genuine sense of achievement when you perfectly nail getting into place first time.

As you make your way to crabbing spots, you’ll also find question mark icons on the map which contain secret stashes that add to the mystery and sometimes provide keys or notes that progress the meta-story. It’s entirely possible to just go through and focus on catching crabs, but that only scratches the surface of what the game has to offer. Bonus crabpots can also be picked up from these question marks, which enable you to be more efficient in your work.

As mentioned above, every so often, a larger crustacean will board your boat and attack either you or your machinery. You are armed with an axe and a shotgun to fend these foes off, whilst any damage they do must be repaired with a blowtorch. Managing the rhythm of navigation, catching crabs, defeating enemies, and maintaining the boat soon fits into a satisfying loop – one that sees your debt slowly decreasing along with the time limit.

Summary

Crabmeat is a great short-form horror game that delves deep into the existential dread of both being in debt and being stranded in an unforgiving sea. The challenge and threat amps up throughout the 3-hour playtime and everything culminates in a conclusion that works as a suitably fantastic payoff. Similar to a game like Threshold, this is a one-sitting experience that shows the range of indie horror gaming.

Good

  • Innovative controls
  • Atmospheric and bleak
  • Intuitive mechanics

Bad

  • Some may bounce off the mouse controls


https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/13/crabmeat-review-claws-and-effect/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Roguelite spin-off Deep Rock Galactic: Rogue Core digs up an Early Access release date
item.content:encoded:
Ghost Ship Games’ hit sci-fi dwarf mining series will get bigger in a couple months, with Deep Rock Galactic: Rogue Core set to launch into Steam Early Access on 20th May 2026.

This standalone game puts a roguelite spin on the popular 4 player co-op action. Check out the trailer:

Where Deep Rock Galactic is all about getting in, mining and finding what you need and getting back out alive, Deep Rock Galactic: Rogue Core skips that pesky getting out alive part.

Hoxxes IV is now in a looming state of shutdown, with mine sites cut off from the outside world. The only solution the Deep Rock Galactic Mining Corporation can come up with is to deploy its elite security team, the Reclaimers, to go and five hundred miles, and then walk five hundred more, descending into the depths to reach and reinstate dig sites.

There’s five playable classes – Guardian, Spotter, Falconer, Slicer, and Retcon – each with strengths and abilities, and the game will now have a roguelite loop, starting of with basic gear and working to level it up through a run and through metaprogression.

There’s new gear, compared to the main game, including a zipline launcher traversal tool, the Zhukov NUK17 submachine gun, and plenty of grenades. Oh, and there’s new creatures, such as the Corespawn, Rafkan, and Shatterclaw.

Elsewhere in the DRG-verse, Deep Rock Galactic: Survivor has a major new expansion on its own journey through Early Access. The Demolisher arrives in a powerful mining vehicle that (in a shocking twist for a mining game spin-off) mine through the scenery in this bullet heaven Survivors-like.

The Deep Rock Galactic: Survivor – Heavy Duty expansion will arrive on 30th April.

Source: press releases



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/13/roguelite-spin-off-deep-rock-galactic-rogue-core-digs-up-an-early-access-release-date/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: CALX is a new sci-fi action adventure game with a gorgeous art style
item.content:encoded:
It does seem like there’s a game announcement show every couple of hours, but, if you like video games, is that really a bad thing? This week, it’s been the Future Games Show, and amongst the announcements, one of the ones that really stood out to me was CALX from True Colors, published by Dear Villagers. This sci-fi adventure game looks absolutely stunning, and, in a nice surprise to everyone, you don’t have to wait for very long to check it out, with the game releasing on the 4th of June, 2026.

You control the Seeker, a little knight-like chap, who sets out to explore the planet of Syro, investigating what happened to the Quoth civilisation at the hands of the Warp. It’s a classic-sounding sci-fi setup, but the reason you’re going to be drawn to CALX is surely the unbelievable artstyle.

There’s a shared visual language with games like Sable and Rollerdrome, with its retrofuturistic art design likely to draw in crowds of players alone. The gameplay itself looks slightly more traditional, but I can’t wait to slice my way through the alien enemies and do some 3D platforming while exploring every inch of the gorgeous planet that True Colors have created.

True Colors are an indie developer based in Turin, Italy and were founded in 2022. CALX is their first game, and, interestingly, the game must have recently undergone a change of name, as the team’s website list it as XTAL instead. I guess, that’s still got most of the letters in it?

If you’re interested in finding out more about CALX, there’s currently a demo available on Steam right now, so you can go explore a bit of Syro for yourself, and immerse yourself in those incredible visuals. The demo includes around an hour of gameplay too, so there’s plenty to sink your teeth into!



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/13/calx-is-a-new-sci-fi-action-adventure-game-with-a-gorgeous-art-style/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: This little piggy’s Adorable Adventures will start with an April release date
item.content:encoded:
Wild Sheep Studio and publisher PQube have announced that Adorable Adventures will bring the adventure of Boris the baby boar to PS5, Xbox Series X|S and PC on 30th April

How adorable is that? Boris has got separated from his family in a forest fire, but can set off to try and track them down. Boris will have to learn how to use his sense of smell to navigate – picking up scents of flowers, mushrooms, honeycomb and more – and have to start peeling away the strongest smells to pick out more subtle notes and find his brothers and sisters. Each of his six siblings will be able to join Boris on his continuing adventures, but you’ll need to gee them up a little. His sleepy brother, Gary, needs a bit of energy (classic Gary), while little sister Mimi wants to play hide and seek, but might get herself into trouble as she does so.

He will be accompanied by the voiceover of park ranger Maxime, whose storytelling will adapt to what you do. You can also find journal entries from maxime’s journal and give Boris some accessories. But he’s still a baby boar, and easily distracted. The game will also have time trial races, photography challenges, a spot of park tidying to do, and a local mystery to solve.

Wild Sheep Studio has form in this tone of game. The studio was originally founded to work on WILD with Michel Ancel and Ubisoft Paris in 2013, but after that game’s cancellation shifted to smaller games. Adorable Adventures has seen them head out from the studio to explore the Cévennes National Park, which is close to their base in Southern France, and take 3D scans of local plant life and ambient field recordings to recreate the forest atmosphere.

Source: Press release



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/13/this-little-piggys-adorable-adventures-will-start-with-an-april-release-date/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Dave the Diver: In the Jungle DLC gets a June release date
item.content:encoded:
Mintrocket has announced that the In the Jungle DLC for Dave the Diver will launch on 18th June for both PC and consoles, expanding the game with new freshwater diving, more minigames and new characters to meet.

In the Jungle sees Dave diving into new territory, heading to the jungle village of Utara where time flows in real-time and with a new restaurant to stock.

Dave will go diving underwater, gather fruits and resources, while building relationships with the locals by completing quests. The freshwater lake of Blue Hole is the new diving location, with new aquatic life, possible cuisine, and (of course) new dangers. To help his ventures, Dave’s now got the Jungle Gun, which can transform into net guns, shotguns, snipers, or regular guns.

This all helps to boost the new Bancho Grill restaurant with a fully cooked freshwater menu. In this restaurant, Dave can now move around more freely and sever customers in multiple areas instead of along a single plane.

Also announced today is the upcoming release of a Dave the Diver physical edition for PS5 and Nintendo Switch 2, in partnership with Silver Lining Direct. The Dave the Diver Collector’s Edition will feature all the DLC up to and including In the Jungle on a single disc or cartridge, in addition to some physical goodies:

  • Bancho Sushi Restaurant Acrylic Standee
  • 4 Metal Pin Badges
  • Acrylic Keyring
  • Dolphin Pendant Necklace
  • 2 Marinca Cards
  • Poster
  • 4 Postcards

Source: press release



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/13/dave-the-diver-in-the-jungle-dlc-gets-a-june-release-date/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
